explanation: Digestion begins in the mouth (mechanical and chemical), continues in the stomach (acid and enzymes), and is completed in the small intestine where most absorption of nutrients occurs.

subject: science
question: What is the function of the kidneys?
options: To produce hormones only
To filter blood, remove waste, and regulate fluid and electrolyte balance by producing urine
To digest proteins
To pump blood through the body
explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove metabolic waste (urea, excess salts, water), producing urine; they also regulate blood pressure, electrolyte balance, and red blood cell production.

subject: science
question: What are neurons?
options: A type of muscle cell
Specialized cells of the nervous system that transmit electrical signals (nerve impulses)
Cells in the immune system
Blood cells that carry oxygen
explanation: Neurons are the specialized cells of the nervous system that transmit electrical signals (nerve impulses) and chemical signals (neurotransmitters) throughout the body.

subject: science
question: What is the function of white blood cells?
options: To carry oxygen
To help clot blood
To defend the body against pathogens and infection as part of the immune system
To transport nutrients
explanation: White blood cells (leukocytes) are part of the immune system; they identify and destroy pathogens (bacteria, viruses), produce antibodies, and remove dead cells and debris.

subject: science
question: What is the endocrine system?
options: The system of blood vessels in the body
The system of glands that produce hormones to regulate body functions
The system of muscles attached to the skeleton
The system of organs involved in digestion
explanation: The endocrine system consists of glands (pituitary, thyroid, adrenal, pancreas, etc.) that produce hormones — chemical messengers that regulate growth, metabolism, reproduction, and other body functions.

subject: science
question: What is the function of the liver?
options: Only to produce bile for digestion
To detoxify blood, produce bile, metabolize nutrients, synthesize proteins, and store glycogen
Only to regulate blood sugar
To filter urine
explanation: The liver performs over 500 functions including detoxifying blood, producing bile (for fat digestion), metabolizing carbohydrates, fats, and proteins, synthesizing clotting factors, and storing glycogen.

subject: science
question: What is the immune system's role?
options: To digest food
To pump blood
To defend the body against diseases caused by pathogens, using white blood cells, antibodies, and other defenses
To regulate body temperature
explanation: The immune system defends the body against pathogens (bacteria, viruses, parasites) using physical barriers (skin), non-specific defenses (inflammation), and specific responses (antibodies, T cells).

subject: science
question: What is the function of the pancreas?
options: To filter blood
To produce insulin (to lower blood sugar) and glucagon (to raise blood sugar), and digestive enzymes
To store bile
To produce red blood cells
explanation: The pancreas has two main roles: it produces hormones (insulin and glucagon) to regulate blood sugar levels, and it produces digestive enzymes released into the small intestine.

subject: science
question: How many bones are in the adult human body?
explanation: The adult human skeleton has 206 bones; babies are born with about 270-300 bones, but many fuse together as the body develops through childhood and adolescence.

subject: science
question: What is homeostasis?
options: The growth of new tissues
The ability of the body to maintain stable internal conditions (like temperature and blood sugar) despite external changes
The process of cellular respiration
The coordination of voluntary movements
explanation: Homeostasis is the maintenance of stable internal conditions (temperature, pH, blood glucose, water balance) within narrow limits; it involves feedback mechanisms using the nervous and endocrine systems.

subject: science
question: What is the function of the large intestine?
options: To digest proteins and fats
To absorb water from undigested food, compact waste into feces, and expel it
To produce digestive enzymes
To absorb most nutrients from food
explanation: The large intestine (colon) absorbs water and electrolytes from remaining indigestible food, compacts the material into feces, and stores it until elimination.

subject: science
question: What type of joint allows the widest range of movement?
options: Hinge joint
Pivot joint
Ball-and-socket joint
Gliding joint
explanation: Ball-and-socket joints (like the hip and shoulder) allow movement in all directions — flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, and rotation — giving the widest range of motion.

subject: science
question: What is the function of the diaphragm?
options: To pump blood to the lungs
A dome-shaped muscle below the lungs that contracts to expand the chest cavity and enable breathing
To support the stomach
To filter blood entering the heart
explanation: The diaphragm is the main breathing muscle; when it contracts, it flattens and expands the thoracic cavity, reducing air pressure and causing air to rush into the lungs (inhalation).

subject: science
question: What are the four chambers of the human heart?
options: Two atria and two ventricles
One atrium and three ventricles
Three atria and one ventricle
Two atria and one ventricle
explanation: The human heart has four chambers: the right atrium and right ventricle (pumping blood to the lungs) and the left atrium and left ventricle (pumping oxygenated blood to the body).

subject: science
question: What is blood pressure?
options: The speed of blood flow through veins
The force that blood exerts against the walls of blood vessels, measured in mmHg
The number of red blood cells per milliliter
The oxygen content of blood
explanation: Blood pressure is the force exerted by blood against blood vessel walls; it is measured as systolic (heart contracting) over diastolic (heart relaxing) pressure in mmHg, normally about 120/80.

subject: science
question: What is the role of tendons?
options: Connect bone to bone
Connect muscle to bone, transmitting the force of muscle contraction to move bones
Cushion joints
Carry electrical signals to muscles
explanation: Tendons are tough bands of connective tissue that connect muscles to bones; they transmit the pulling force generated by muscle contraction to move bones at joints.

subject: science
question: What is the role of ligaments?
options: Connect muscle to bone
Connect bone to bone at joints, providing stability
Carry nerve signals
Cushion bones from impact
explanation: Ligaments are fibrous connective tissue bands that connect bones to other bones at joints; they stabilize joints and prevent excessive or abnormal movement.

subject: science
question: What is the central nervous system (CNS)?
options: All the nerves in the arms and legs
The brain and spinal cord, which process and integrate information
The nerves connecting organs to the brain
The part of the nervous system controlling involuntary actions
explanation: The central nervous system (CNS) consists of the brain and spinal cord; it processes sensory information, integrates it, and coordinates responses sent via the peripheral nervous system.

subject: science
question: What is the function of cartilage?
options: To produce blood cells
To provide flexible support and cushion joints, preventing bone-on-bone friction
To carry electrical signals
To store energy as fat
explanation: Cartilage is flexible connective tissue found in joints (as cushioning), the ear, nose, and trachea; it reduces friction between bones and absorbs shock in joints.

subject: science
question: What is the largest organ in the human body?
options: The liver
The lungs
The brain
The skin
explanation: The skin is the largest organ in the human body; it covers about 2 square meters and serves as a barrier against pathogens, regulates temperature, and contains sensory receptors.

subject: science
question: What is the function of the small intestine?
options: To store bile
To absorb most of the nutrients from digested food into the bloodstream
To compact feces
To produce stomach acid
explanation: The small intestine (about 6-7 meters long) is the main site of nutrient absorption; enzymes from the pancreas and bile from the liver complete digestion, and nutrients are absorbed through villi into the blood.

subject: science
question: What is the role of the spinal cord?
options: To produce cerebrospinal fluid only
To relay signals between the brain and the body, and to process reflexes
To control voluntary movements only
To regulate hormone production
explanation: The spinal cord relays nerve signals between the brain and the peripheral nervous system; it also processes reflex arcs, allowing rapid involuntary responses without involving the brain.

subject: science
question: What is the function of platelets?
options: To carry oxygen
To fight infection
To help form blood clots and stop bleeding when blood vessels are damaged
To produce antibodies
explanation: Platelets (thrombocytes) are small cell fragments that aggregate at wound sites and participate in the clotting cascade, forming a plug to stop bleeding and initiate healing.

subject: science
question: What is the function of the cerebrum?
options: To control heartbeat and breathing
The largest part of the brain responsible for conscious thought, voluntary movement, sensory processing, memory, and language
To regulate balance and coordination
To relay signals between brain regions
explanation: The cerebrum is the largest brain region; its cerebral cortex processes sensory information, controls voluntary movements, and is responsible for higher functions like reasoning, language, and memory.

subject: science
question: What are arteries, veins, and capillaries?
options: Three types of blood cells
Arteries carry blood away from the heart; veins carry blood toward the heart; capillaries are tiny vessels where gas and nutrient exchange occurs
Types of heart valves
Different parts of the lymphatic system
explanation: Arteries carry oxygenated blood away from the heart; veins carry deoxygenated blood back to the heart; capillaries are microscopic vessels in tissues where oxygen, nutrients, and waste exchange occurs.

subject: science
question: What is the role of the lymphatic system?
options: To circulate blood
To return excess interstitial fluid to the bloodstream, filter lymph, and support immune function
To digest fats only
To regulate breathing
explanation: The lymphatic system collects excess fluid from tissues and returns it to the blood, filters lymph through lymph nodes (removing pathogens), and plays a key role in immune responses.

subject: science
question: What is the function of the stomach?
options: To absorb nutrients into the blood
To mechanically and chemically break down food using acid and enzymes, producing chyme
To produce bile for fat digestion
To absorb water from food
explanation: The stomach mechanically churns food and chemically digests proteins using hydrochloric acid (HCl) and the enzyme pepsin, producing a semi-liquid mixture called chyme.

subject: science
question: What is the role of insulin?
options: To increase blood sugar levels
A hormone produced by the pancreas that lowers blood glucose by promoting its uptake by cells
To digest carbohydrates
To regulate water levels in the body
explanation: Insulin is a hormone produced by pancreatic beta cells that signals body cells (especially liver and muscle) to take up glucose from the blood, lowering blood glucose levels after meals.

subject: science
question: What is the function of the cerebellum?
options: To control conscious thought
To coordinate movement, balance, and fine motor skills
To regulate hunger and thirst
To produce hormones
explanation: The cerebellum coordinates voluntary movements, maintains balance and posture, and fine-tunes motor skills; damage to it causes loss of coordination (ataxia).

subject: science
question: What is the role of the skin in temperature regulation?
options: The skin plays no role in temperature regulation
The skin regulates temperature through sweating (cooling) and shivering, and by controlling blood flow to the skin surface
The skin only prevents water loss
The skin raises body temperature through metabolism
explanation: The skin regulates body temperature: sweat glands produce sweat that cools the body through evaporation; blood vessels in the skin dilate (releasing heat) or constrict (retaining heat) as needed.

subject: science
question: What is the difference between voluntary and involuntary muscles?
options: They are the same type of muscle
Voluntary muscles (skeletal) are consciously controlled; involuntary muscles (smooth and cardiac) operate automatically without conscious control
Involuntary muscles are found only in the heart
Voluntary muscles are only found in the face
explanation: Voluntary (skeletal) muscles are consciously controlled for movement; involuntary muscles include smooth muscles (in organs and blood vessels) and cardiac muscle (heart), which operate automatically.

subject: science
question: What is the function of the esophagus?
options: To produce digestive enzymes
A muscular tube that transports food from the mouth to the stomach through peristalsis
To store food before digestion
To absorb nutrients from food
explanation: The esophagus is a muscular tube connecting the mouth to the stomach; it moves food downward through wave-like muscle contractions called peristalsis.

subject: science
question: What is a reflex arc?
options: A voluntary conscious response to a stimulus
An automatic, rapid response to a stimulus processed through the spinal cord without waiting for the brain
The pathway nerve signals take to reach the brain
The reflex movements of the eyes
explanation: A reflex arc is a neural pathway that produces a rapid, automatic response to a stimulus; it bypasses the brain by processing the response in the spinal cord, allowing faster reaction times.

subject: science
question: What are the major types of muscle tissue?
options: Smooth, rough, and cardiac
Skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle
Fast and slow muscle fibers only
Voluntary and sensory muscle
explanation: The three types of muscle tissue are: skeletal (voluntary, striated, attached to bones), smooth (involuntary, in organs and blood vessels), and cardiac (involuntary, forms the heart wall).

subject: science
question: What is the role of the thyroid gland?
options: To regulate blood sugar levels
To produce hormones (thyroxine) that regulate metabolism, growth, and energy use
To produce insulin
To regulate reproductive hormones
explanation: The thyroid gland (in the neck) produces thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3), hormones that regulate metabolic rate, energy production, growth, and development throughout the body.

subject: science
question: What is the function of sweat glands?
options: To produce oil to protect the skin
To cool the body through evaporative cooling when sweat evaporates from the skin surface
To produce waterproofing for the skin
To detect temperature changes
explanation: Sweat glands produce sweat (mainly water and salts) that is secreted onto the skin surface; as sweat evaporates, it removes heat from the body, cooling the skin and maintaining body temperature.

subject: science
question: What is the function of the gallbladder?
options: To produce bile
To store and concentrate bile produced by the liver, releasing it into the small intestine when needed for fat digestion
To filter blood
To produce digestive enzymes
explanation: The gallbladder stores and concentrates bile (produced by the liver); when fatty food enters the small intestine, the gallbladder releases bile through the bile duct to emulsify fats for digestion.

subject: science
question: What are the main functions of the respiratory system?
options: To circulate blood and regulate temperature
To bring oxygen into the body and remove carbon dioxide, while also regulating blood pH
To filter waste from blood
To digest food and absorb nutrients
explanation: The respiratory system brings oxygen into the body through inhalation, exchanges gases in the lungs (O2 in, CO2 out), and expels CO2 during exhalation; it also helps regulate blood pH.

subject: science
question: What is the role of saliva in digestion?
options: To kill bacteria in food
To begin starch digestion (with amylase), moisten food into a bolus, and lubricate the esophagus
To break down proteins
To neutralize stomach acid
explanation: Saliva contains the enzyme amylase (which begins starch digestion), mucus (which lubricates food), and water; it forms food into a moist bolus for easy swallowing.

subject: science
question: What is the function of bone marrow?
options: To store minerals only
Red bone marrow produces red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets; yellow marrow stores fat
To produce hormones
To provide structural rigidity to bones
explanation: Red bone marrow is the site of hematopoiesis (blood cell production), creating red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets; yellow marrow is primarily fat storage.

subject: science
question: What is the function of the brain stem?
options: To control conscious thought
To control automatic vital functions like breathing, heartbeat, and blood pressure
To coordinate voluntary movement
To regulate hunger and thirst
explanation: The brain stem (medulla oblongata, pons, and midbrain) controls automatic, vital functions including breathing, heart rate, blood pressure, and reflexes like swallowing and coughing.

subject: science
question: What is the role of the reproductive system?
options: To regulate hormone levels only
To produce sex cells (gametes) and, in females, to support the development of offspring
To regulate the immune system
To provide structural support to the body
explanation: The reproductive system produces gametes (sperm in males, eggs in females), enables fertilization, and in females, supports the development of offspring during pregnancy.

subject: science
question: What is the function of the hypothalamus?
options: To process visual information
A region of the brain that regulates homeostasis, including body temperature, hunger, thirst, sleep, and hormone release from the pituitary gland
To control voluntary movement
To produce digestive enzymes
explanation: The hypothalamus is the body's master regulator; it monitors and controls temperature, hunger, thirst, sleep cycles, and controls the pituitary gland, linking the nervous and endocrine systems.

subject: science
question: What is the difference between aerobic and anaerobic respiration in muscles?
options: They are the same process
Aerobic respiration uses oxygen to produce ATP efficiently; anaerobic respiration occurs without oxygen, producing less ATP and lactic acid
Anaerobic respiration only occurs in the lungs
Aerobic respiration produces lactic acid
explanation: Aerobic cellular respiration uses oxygen to fully break down glucose into CO2, water, and large amounts of ATP; anaerobic respiration (in oxygen-deprived muscles) produces ATP quickly but inefficiently, with lactic acid as a byproduct.

subject: science
question: What is electric current?
options: The voltage across a circuit
The flow of electric charge (electrons) through a conductor
The resistance of a material to electric flow
The power consumed by an electrical device
explanation: Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charge (electrons) through a conductor; it is measured in amperes (A) using an ammeter.

subject: science
question: What is voltage?
options: The flow of electrons through a wire
The electrical potential difference that drives current through a circuit, measured in volts (V)
The opposition to current flow
The energy stored in a capacitor
explanation: Voltage (electric potential difference) is the driving force that pushes electric current through a circuit; it is analogous to water pressure and is measured in volts (V).

subject: science
question: What is resistance in an electric circuit?
options: The flow rate of electrons
The opposition to the flow of electric current in a conductor, measured in ohms (Ω)
The rate of energy transfer
The charge stored in a battery
explanation: Resistance is the opposition to the flow of electric current; materials with high resistance impede current flow more; it is measured in ohms (Ω) and depends on material, length, and cross-section.

subject: science
question: What does Ohm's Law state?
options: Power equals force times distance
Voltage equals current times resistance (V = IR)
Current equals power divided by voltage
Resistance equals voltage times current
explanation: Ohm's Law states V = IR: voltage equals current multiplied by resistance; this fundamental relationship allows calculation of any one variable when the other two are known.

subject: science
question: What is the difference between a series circuit and a parallel circuit?
options: They are the same type of circuit
In series, components are connected in a single path; in parallel, components have separate paths for current
Series circuits have more voltage; parallel circuits have more current
In parallel, all components share the same current
explanation: In a series circuit, components are connected end-to-end in one path; if one fails, all fail. In a parallel circuit, each component has its own current path; if one fails, others continue working.

subject: science
question: What is a conductor?
options: A material that resists electric current
A material that allows electric current to flow easily through it
A material that stores electric charge
A device that measures current
explanation: A conductor is a material that allows electrons to flow easily; metals like copper, silver, and aluminum are excellent conductors because they have many free (loosely bound) electrons.

subject: science
question: What is an insulator?
options: A material that conducts electricity well
A material that resists the flow of electric current
A material that stores electrical energy
A device that increases voltage
explanation: An insulator is a material that does not allow electrons to flow easily; rubber, glass, plastic, and wood are good insulators because their electrons are tightly bound to atoms.

subject: science
question: What is electric power?
options: The total energy in a circuit
The rate at which electrical energy is converted to another form, P = IV, measured in watts (W)
The voltage of a power source
The resistance of electrical components
explanation: Electric power is the rate of energy conversion in an electrical device; P = IV (power = current × voltage); it is measured in watts (W), where 1 W = 1 J/s.

subject: science
question: What is static electricity?
options: Electric current flowing through wires
The buildup of electric charge on the surface of an object, which can discharge suddenly
Electricity produced by magnets
Electricity stored in capacitors
explanation: Static electricity is the buildup of electric charge on an object's surface (usually from friction); the charge can discharge suddenly (like a spark or lightning) when it finds a path to flow.

subject: science
question: What causes lightning?
options: Friction between rain clouds
The discharge of static electricity built up between clouds or between clouds and the ground
Magnetic fields in thunderstorms
Radio waves from thunderstorms
explanation: Lightning is a massive electrostatic discharge; static charge builds up in storm clouds through ice particle collisions, and when the potential difference is great enough, a discharge (lightning bolt) occurs.

subject: science
question: What is a circuit breaker?
options: A device that measures current
A safety device that automatically interrupts current flow when it exceeds a safe level, preventing overheating and fires
A device that stores electrical energy
A device that steps up voltage
explanation: A circuit breaker is a safety switch that automatically trips (opens the circuit) when current exceeds a safe threshold; it protects wiring from overheating and can be reset, unlike a fuse.

subject: science
question: What is the relationship between electricity and magnetism?
options: Electricity and magnetism are completely unrelated
Moving electric charges create magnetic fields, and changing magnetic fields induce electric currents — they are two aspects of electromagnetism
Only permanent magnets produce electric current
Electric current cannot create magnetic fields
explanation: Electricity and magnetism are closely related: moving electric charges produce magnetic fields (electromagnets), and changing magnetic fields induce electric current (generators); together they form electromagnetism.

subject: science
question: What is an electromagnet?
options: A permanent magnet made of iron
A magnet created by passing electric current through a wire coil, which can be turned on and off
A naturally occurring magnetic rock
A type of magnet found in computer hard drives only
explanation: An electromagnet is created by wrapping wire around a ferromagnetic core and passing current through it; the magnetic field exists only when current flows, making it controllable.

subject: science
question: What unit is used to measure electric charge?
options: Volt
Ampere
Coulomb
Ohm
explanation: Electric charge is measured in coulombs (C); one coulomb is the charge of approximately 6.24 × 10^18 electrons; current (amperes) is one coulomb of charge flowing per second.

subject: science
question: What is a semiconductor?
options: A material that conducts better than insulators but worse than good conductors; conductivity can be controlled
A perfect electrical conductor
A material that has no electrical conductivity
A device that converts AC to DC
explanation: Semiconductors (like silicon and germanium) have conductivity between conductors and insulators; their conductivity can be precisely controlled, making them essential for transistors and computer chips.

subject: science
question: What is a fuse in an electric circuit?
options: A device that increases current
A safety device containing a thin wire that melts and breaks the circuit if current exceeds a safe level
A device that stores charge
A switch that can be reset after tripping
explanation: A fuse contains a thin wire that melts and permanently breaks the circuit if current exceeds its rated value; it protects against short circuits and overloads, but must be replaced once blown.

subject: science
question: In a series circuit, what happens to the total resistance when more resistors are added?
options: Total resistance decreases
Total resistance stays the same
Total resistance increases
Total resistance becomes zero
explanation: In a series circuit, resistors add directly: Rtotal = R1 + R2 + R3 + ...; adding more resistors increases total resistance, which (by Ohm's Law) reduces current in the circuit.

subject: science
question: What is alternating current (AC) vs. direct current (DC)?
options: They are the same type of current
AC reverses direction periodically; DC flows in one constant direction
DC changes direction; AC flows in one direction
AC is used in batteries; DC is used in homes
explanation: Direct current (DC, from batteries) flows in one constant direction; alternating current (AC, from power outlets) reverses direction periodically (50 or 60 times per second), making it efficient for long-distance transmission.

subject: science
question: What is electrical energy?
options: The resistance of a wire to current
Energy carried by electric charges flowing through a circuit, which can be converted to heat, light, motion, or other forms
The voltage of a power source
The rate at which current flows
explanation: Electrical energy is the energy associated with moving electric charges; it is easily converted to other forms (light in a bulb, heat in a toaster, motion in a motor), making it extremely useful.

subject: science
question: What is the function of a battery?
options: To increase the resistance of a circuit
To convert chemical energy into electrical energy, providing a voltage (potential difference) to drive current
To store electrical charge directly
To convert electrical energy into chemical energy only
explanation: A battery converts chemical energy into electrical energy through electrochemical reactions; it creates a potential difference (voltage) between its terminals that drives current through a circuit.

subject: science
question: What is grounding in electrical safety?
options: Connecting a circuit to a voltage source
Connecting an electrical system to the Earth, providing a safe path for excess current in case of a fault
Increasing the resistance of a circuit
Adding a fuse to protect a circuit
explanation: Grounding connects an electrical system to the Earth; if there is a fault (short circuit), excess current flows safely into the ground rather than through a person, preventing electrocution.

subject: science
question: What happens to the total voltage in a series circuit?
options: It is the same across all components
It equals the voltage of one component
The total voltage equals the sum of voltages across each component
Voltage is zero in a series circuit
explanation: In a series circuit, the total voltage of the source equals the sum of the voltage drops across each component (Vtotal = V1 + V2 + V3), as per Kirchhoff's Voltage Law.

subject: science
question: What is the kilowatt-hour (kWh)?
options: A unit of power
A unit of electrical energy equal to the energy used by a 1 kilowatt device for 1 hour
A unit of voltage
A unit of current
explanation: A kilowatt-hour (kWh) is the unit of electrical energy used by electric utilities; it equals the energy used by a 1,000-watt device running for one hour; it is how electricity is billed.

subject: science
question: What is a short circuit?
options: A very short wire in a circuit
An unintended low-resistance connection that allows large current to flow, potentially causing overheating or fire
A circuit with too many resistors
A circuit that uses too little voltage
explanation: A short circuit occurs when current finds a low-resistance path that bypasses the normal circuit; the resulting large current causes excessive heat, and can start fires or damage equipment.

subject: science
question: What is the unit of power?
options: Joule
Volt
Watt
Coulomb
explanation: The watt (W) is the SI unit of power; 1 watt equals 1 joule per second (1 J/s); in electricity, P = IV (power = current × voltage).

subject: science
question: What is an electric field?
options: A region around a magnetic pole
A region around a charged object where another charge would experience a force
The current flowing through a wire
The voltage across a resistor
explanation: An electric field is the region around an electric charge where another charge would experience a force; field lines point from positive to negative charges and show the direction of force on a positive test charge.

subject: science
question: What happens in a parallel circuit if one component fails?
options: All components stop working
The other components continue to work because each has its own current path
The voltage across all components drops to zero
The current in all other components decreases to zero
explanation: In a parallel circuit, each component has its own branch; if one branch fails, current still flows through the other branches; this is why home wiring uses parallel circuits.

subject: science
question: What is a diode?
options: A device that measures resistance
A semiconductor device that allows current to flow in only one direction
A device that stores electrical energy
A coil used to create a magnetic field
explanation: A diode is a semiconductor component that allows current to flow in only one direction; it is used to convert AC to DC (rectification) and in LED lights and solar cells.

subject: science
question: What is the law of conservation of energy as applied to electrical circuits?
options: Energy is created in the battery
Energy supplied by the source equals energy consumed by all components in the circuit
Energy increases as it moves through a circuit
Resistors create new energy from heat
explanation: Energy conservation in circuits (Kirchhoff's laws) requires that all energy provided by the source is consumed by circuit components; no energy is created or destroyed.

subject: science
question: What is the function of a switch in an electric circuit?
options: To increase the voltage
To open or close the circuit, controlling whether current flows
To reduce the resistance
To store electrical energy
explanation: A switch opens or closes an electric circuit; when open, it breaks the circuit and stops current flow; when closed, it completes the circuit and allows current to flow.

subject: science
question: What is the unit of resistance?
options: Volt
Ampere
Watt
Ohm
explanation: The ohm (Ω) is the SI unit of electrical resistance; named after Georg Ohm, one ohm equals one volt per ampere (1 Ω = 1 V/A).

subject: science
question: How does doubling the voltage affect current in a circuit with constant resistance?
options: Current is halved
Current stays the same
Current is doubled
Current increases by a factor of four
explanation: By Ohm's Law (I = V/R), if resistance is constant and voltage doubles, current doubles; this direct proportionality between voltage and current is a fundamental relationship in circuits.

subject: science
question: What is a capacitor?
options: A device that creates current
A device that stores electrical energy in an electric field between two conducting plates
A type of resistor
A device that converts electrical energy to sound
explanation: A capacitor stores electrical energy in an electric field between two parallel conducting plates separated by an insulator; it can release stored energy quickly and is used in filters and timing circuits.

subject: science
question: What safety precautions should be taken when working with electricity?
options: Water conducts electricity, so working in water is safe
Never touch live wires, use insulated tools, keep water away from electrical equipment, and use proper grounding
Electrical current cannot harm humans
Only high-voltage electricity is dangerous
explanation: Electrical safety requires: never touching bare live wires, using insulated tools, keeping water away from electrical equipment, using circuit breakers and fuses, and proper grounding to prevent electrocution.

subject: science
question: What is the effect of temperature on resistance in most metals?
options: Resistance decreases as temperature increases
Resistance increases as temperature increases
Temperature has no effect on resistance
Resistance becomes zero at high temperatures
explanation: In most metals, resistance increases with temperature; higher temperatures cause atoms to vibrate more, increasing collisions with electrons and impeding current flow (superconductors are the notable exception at very low temperatures).

subject: science
question: What is an LED?
options: A type of battery
A light-emitting diode that converts electrical energy directly into light
A device that measures current
A type of capacitor
explanation: An LED (light-emitting diode) is a semiconductor device that emits light when current flows through it; LEDs are energy-efficient (converting most electrical energy to light, not heat) and long-lasting.

subject: science
question: What is a transformer used for?
options: To store electrical energy
To change (step up or step down) AC voltage levels using electromagnetic induction
To convert AC to DC
To measure voltage in a circuit
explanation: A transformer uses electromagnetic induction (changing magnetic fields in coils) to step up (increase) or step down (decrease) AC voltage; power companies use step-up transformers to transmit electricity at high voltage over long distances.

subject: science
question: What is Joule heating?
options: The cooling of a conductor when current flows
The production of heat in a conductor due to the resistance to current flow (P = I²R)
The heating of insulators by magnetic fields
The heat produced by a capacitor
explanation: Joule heating (resistive heating) is the heat produced when current flows through a resistor (P = I²R); it explains why electric heaters and incandescent light bulbs become hot.

subject: science
question: What determines the direction of current flow in a circuit?
options: The resistance of the resistors
Current flows from the negative terminal to positive terminal (electron flow), or by convention from positive to negative (conventional current)
Current flows randomly in circuits
The color of the wires
explanation: By convention, current flows from + to - terminal (conventional current); electron flow is actually from - to +; current always flows from higher to lower electrical potential through the external circuit.

subject: science
question: What is a generator?
options: A device that stores electrical energy
A device that converts mechanical energy into electrical energy using electromagnetic induction
A device that measures power consumption
A type of battery
explanation: An electric generator converts mechanical energy into electrical energy; rotating a coil within a magnetic field induces an alternating current (AC) by electromagnetic induction, powering homes and businesses.

subject: science
question: What is an electric motor?
options: A device that stores charge
A device that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy using magnetic forces on current-carrying conductors
A type of battery
A device that increases voltage
explanation: An electric motor converts electrical energy into mechanical energy; current-carrying conductors in a magnetic field experience forces (Lorentz force) that cause rotation, driving machines and vehicles.

subject: science
question: What is the difference between voltage and current?
options: They are the same thing
Voltage is the potential difference (driving force) that pushes current; current is the actual flow of charge through a circuit
Current creates voltage; voltage does not cause current
Voltage is measured in amperes; current in volts
explanation: Voltage (V) is the electrical potential difference — the 'pressure' that drives current; current (I) is the resulting flow of charge through the circuit; voltage is the cause, current is the effect.

subject: science
question: What is the principle behind a solar cell (photovoltaic cell)?
options: Mechanical spinning generates electricity
Light photons knock electrons free in a semiconductor, creating a voltage and current
Heat from the sun warms a fluid that turns a turbine
Chemical reactions in sunlight produce electricity
explanation: Solar cells use the photovoltaic effect: photons of light knock electrons free in a semiconductor (silicon), creating a flow of electrons (current) that can be harnessed as electricity.

subject: science
question: What is the purpose of the three prongs on many electrical plugs?
options: The three prongs only fit certain outlets
Two prongs carry current (hot and neutral); the third is a ground wire for safety
The third prong increases voltage
Extra prongs reduce resistance in the circuit
explanation: Three-prong plugs have hot (live), neutral, and ground wires; the ground provides a safe path for fault current, protecting users from electrocution if there is a wiring fault.

subject: science
question: What happens to the brightness of bulbs in a series circuit when a bulb burns out?
options: Other bulbs burn brighter
All bulbs go out because the circuit is broken
Other bulbs stay the same brightness
Only the burned out bulb dims
explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current; if one bulb burns out (opens the circuit), current stops flowing through the entire circuit, and all bulbs go out — like old-style Christmas lights.

subject: science
question: How is electricity transmitted from power plants to homes?
options: Through underground water pipes
Through high-voltage transmission lines, with transformers stepping voltage up for transmission and down for home use
Through wireless radio transmission
Directly at 120V from the power plant
explanation: Electricity is generated at power plants, stepped up to very high voltage (reduces current and energy loss), transmitted through power lines, then stepped down by transformers near homes to 120V or 240V for safe use.

subject: science
question: What is electrical resistance influenced by?
options: Only temperature
Material type, length, cross-sectional area, and temperature
Only the length of the conductor
Only the material of the conductor
explanation: Resistance depends on: material (copper resists less than iron), length (longer = more resistance), cross-sectional area (thicker = less resistance), and temperature (generally higher temperature = more resistance in metals).

subject: science
question: What is a voltmeter and how is it connected in a circuit?
options: An instrument measuring current, connected in series
An instrument measuring voltage, connected in parallel across the component being measured
An instrument measuring resistance, connected in series
An instrument measuring power, connected anywhere
explanation: A voltmeter measures electrical potential difference (voltage); it has very high resistance and is connected in parallel (across) the component whose voltage is being measured.

subject: science
question: What is an ammeter and how is it connected?
options: An instrument measuring voltage, connected in parallel
An instrument measuring current, connected in series in the circuit
An instrument measuring resistance, connected across a component
An instrument measuring power, connected to the voltage source
explanation: An ammeter measures electric current; it has very low resistance and must be connected in series (in line with the circuit) so that all current flows through it.

subject: science
question: What is superconductivity?
options: A material with very high resistance at low temperatures
The property of certain materials to conduct electricity with zero resistance at very low temperatures
The ability of metals to conduct electricity at room temperature
A type of semiconductor behavior
explanation: Superconductivity is the phenomenon where certain materials (cooled below a critical temperature) conduct electricity with absolutely zero resistance, allowing current to flow indefinitely without energy loss.
